definition: The valuation of a startup BEFORE new investment is added. If a VC invests $2M at a $8M pre-money valuation, the post-money is $10M, and the VC owns 20%.
formula: Pre-Money Valuation = Post-Money Valuation − Investment Amount
example: Your startup is valued at ₹40 Cr pre-money. Peak XV invests ₹10 Cr. Post-money valuation = ₹50 Cr. Peak XV owns 20%.
founder_tip: Always negotiate pre-money valuation, not ownership percentage. Higher pre-money = less dilution for you.

definition: The valuation of a startup AFTER new investment is added. Post-money = Pre-money + Investment.
formula: Post-Money = Pre-Money Valuation + Investment Amount
example: Pre-money ₹40 Cr + ₹10 Cr investment = ₹50 Cr post-money. Investor owns 10/50 = 20%.
founder_tip: Investors often quote post-money ownership. Always confirm whether a number is pre or post-money.

definition: An investment instrument created by Y Combinator. An investor gives money now in exchange for equity at a FUTURE priced round, at a discount or valuation cap. No interest, no maturity date.
formula: Conversion Price = MIN(Valuation Cap ÷ Total Shares, Priced Round PPS × (1 − Discount))
example: Angel invests ₹50L on a ₹5 Cr cap SAFE. When you raise a priced Seed at ₹20 Cr pre-money, the angel converts at ₹5 Cr cap — getting 4x more shares than new investors.
founder_tip: SAFEs are founder-friendly (no interest accrual, no debt). India uses 'iSAFE' (iSPIRT version). Always model multiple SAFEs converting at once — called a 'SAFE explosion'.

definition: The maximum valuation at which a SAFE or convertible note will convert into equity. Protects early investors from getting diluted if the startup raises at a very high valuation.
formula: Conversion Equity % = Investment ÷ MIN(Cap, Priced Round Pre-Money)
example: Angel invests $100K with $2M cap. You raise at $10M. Without cap, angel gets 1%. With $2M cap, angel gets 5%.
founder_tip: Set your cap 2-5x higher than your current valuation to give yourself room. Multiple SAFEs at different caps can create messy cap tables.

definition: A percentage reduction on the next round's price per share for SAFE/note holders, rewarding them for investing early. Typically 10-25%.
formula: Conversion Price = Priced Round PPS × (1 − Discount%)
example: 20% discount. Priced Seed at ₹100/share. SAFE converts at ₹80/share. Early investor gets 25% more shares than new investors.
founder_tip: Standard discount is 15-20%. Never give both a high cap AND a high discount — pick one form of protection.

definition: A debt instrument that converts to equity at a future financing event. Has interest rate (typically 5-8%) and maturity date (18-24 months). If no conversion event, the debt is due.
formula: Conversion Amount = Principal + Accrued Interest at maturity if no conversion
example: ₹1 Cr note at 8% interest, 18-month maturity, 20% discount. At Seed round, converts with ₹1.12 Cr (with interest) at 20% discount.
founder_tip: Convertible notes are debt — they appear on your balance sheet. SAFE is cleaner for most early-stage Indian startups. RBI regulations limit foreign convertible notes.

definition: Determines who gets paid first and how much in a liquidation event (acquisition, bankruptcy). 1x non-participating is standard; participating preferred is founder-unfriendly.
formula: 1x Non-Participating: Investor gets investment back OR converts to common, whichever is higher. Participating: Investor gets investment back PLUS pro-rata of remaining proceeds.
example: Investor put in $5M. You sell for $10M. With 1x non-participating: investor gets $5M, you get $5M. With 1x participating: investor gets $5M + 30% of remaining $5M = $6.5M. You get $3.5M.
founder_tip: ALWAYS push for 1x non-participating preferred. 2x preferences and participating preferred will kill founder economics in a down/flat exit. This is the most negotiated term.

definition: A liquidation preference structure where investors get their money back FIRST AND THEN participate in remaining proceeds as if they had converted to common. 'Double dipping.'
formula: Investor payout = Liquidation Preference + (Ownership% × Remaining Proceeds)
example: 2x participating preferred. $5M invested. $20M exit. Investor gets $10M (2x) + 20% × $10M remaining = $12M. Founders split $8M despite owning 80%.
founder_tip: Reject participating preferred if possible. If forced, negotiate a cap (e.g., 2x cap) after which it automatically converts to common. PEAK XV and Accel rarely push for participating preferred.

definition: Protects investors from dilution if the company raises future rounds at a lower valuation (down round). Two types: Broad-Based Weighted Average (founder-friendly) and Full Ratchet (very founder-unfriendly).
formula: Broad-Based WA: New Conversion Price = Old Price × (Shares Before Round + Investment/Old Price) / (Shares Before Round + New Shares Issued)
example: Investor paid ₹100/share at Series A. Series B happens at ₹60/share (down round). Full Ratchet: all Series A shares reprice to ₹60 — massive dilution for founders. Broad-Based WA: gentler repricing.
founder_tip: Always accept broad-based weighted average, never full ratchet. Down rounds are rare if you raise at reasonable valuations — don't sign aggressive anti-dilution protection.

definition: The right for existing investors to maintain their ownership percentage in future rounds by investing proportionally. 'Super pro-rata' gives rights to invest more than their current ownership %.
formula: Pro-Rata Amount = New Round Size × Current Ownership %
example: Investor owns 10% after Seed. Company raises Series A. Pro-rata right allows them to invest 10% of the Series A to maintain their 10% position.
founder_tip: Pro-rata is a standard and reasonable term. Super pro-rata limits your ability to bring in new investors. Negotiate expiry (e.g., rights expire after Series B).

definition: A position on the company's Board of Directors. Investors often ask for a board seat at Seed or Series A. Board votes on major decisions: hiring/firing CEO, approval of budgets, M&A, new equity issuances.
formula: N/A
example: Standard Series A board: 2 founders, 1 lead investor, 1 independent. As you raise more rounds, boards grow — try to keep it at 5 or fewer.
founder_tip: Negotiate a board observer seat vs. a full board seat when possible — observer has less voting power. Choose your independent board member carefully — they can break ties.

definition: The right to attend board meetings and receive board materials, but WITHOUT voting rights. Common for smaller investors or early-stage angels.
formula: N/A
example: A ₹25L angel investor asks for board observer rights instead of a seat. They can attend, listen, ask questions — but can't vote.
founder_tip: Observer rights are harmless and keep investors engaged. Offer observers before full board seats to satisfy investor information needs without governance burden.

definition: Contractual rights for investors to receive regular financial and business information: monthly/quarterly reports, audited financials, cap table access.
formula: N/A
example: Standard info rights: monthly management reports within 30 days; quarterly financials within 45 days; audited annuals within 120 days.
founder_tip: Information rights are standard — don't fight them. Pro-tip: build monthly investor updates as a habit regardless; it keeps investors engaged and helps with future fundraises.

definition: The right of majority shareholders to force minority shareholders to join in a sale of the company. Prevents minority holders from blocking an acquisition.
formula: N/A
example: VCs own 60%, founders own 40%. A strategic acquirer wants to buy 100%. Drag-along allows VCs to force founders to sell their shares too, even if founders disagree.
founder_tip: Drag-along is standard but negotiate the threshold carefully (e.g., 70% consent required to drag). Also negotiate founder consent requirements for drags.

definition: The right of minority shareholders (usually investors) to join in on a sale of shares by majority shareholders (usually founders). Ensures investors can exit when founders do.
formula: N/A
example: Founder sells 30% of their stake to a secondary buyer. Tag-along allows investors to sell a proportional amount of their stake at the same price.
founder_tip: Tag-along is standard and reasonable. It prevents you from getting liquidity while investors remain locked in. Accept it.

definition: The right to purchase shares before they are offered to third parties. Investors often have ROFR on founder secondary sales. The company also often has ROFR before investors.
formula: N/A
example: Founder wants to sell ₹1 Cr of shares to a secondary buyer. ROFR: Company has 30 days to match the offer first, then investors have 15 days. Only then can founder sell to third party.
founder_tip: ROFR is standard. If your investors exercise ROFR on every secondary, it limits your ability to get liquidity. Negotiate time windows and waivers for small secondary sales.

definition: The process by which founders and employees earn their shares over time. Standard: 4-year vesting with 1-year cliff.
formula: Monthly Vest = Total Shares / (Vesting Period in Months)
example: Founder has 1,000,000 shares on 4-year vest with 1-year cliff. After year 1: 250,000 vest immediately. Then 20,833 vest monthly for 36 more months.
founder_tip: Vesting protects the company if a co-founder leaves early. Always have vesting for all founders — 'gentlemen's agreements' without vesting have destroyed many co-founder relationships.

definition: The minimum time an employee or founder must work before ANY shares vest. Standard cliff is 12 months (1 year). Before the cliff, 0% vests. On the cliff date, the entire cliff period vests at once.
formula: N/A — binary event on cliff date
example: 1-year cliff. Employee joins Jan 2024. If they leave in Nov 2024 (11 months), they get 0 shares. If they stay until Jan 2025 (12 months), they get 25% of all shares at once.
founder_tip: 1-year cliff is standard. Negotiate acceleration (double-trigger) clauses so employees don't lose unvested shares in an acquisition.

definition: Provisions that allow unvested shares to vest immediately upon certain events. Single-trigger: accelerates on acquisition alone. Double-trigger: accelerates only if acquired AND employment terminated.
formula: N/A
example: Double-trigger acceleration: Company is acquired, you're offered a role but fired 6 months later. Double-trigger causes all remaining unvested shares to vest immediately on termination.
founder_tip: Always negotiate double-trigger acceleration for yourself as a founder. Acquirers often fire founders after an acquisition — double-trigger ensures you get your equity.

definition: A pool of shares reserved for employee equity compensation. Options, not shares directly. Employee gets the right to buy shares at a strike price (usually FMV at grant date).
formula: ESOP Value = (FMV at Exercise - Strike Price) × Number of Options
example: Engineer granted 10,000 options at ₹10 strike. Company raises at ₹100/share. Engineer exercises: buys at ₹10, shares worth ₹100. Gain = ₹90 × 10,000 = ₹9 lakh (pre-tax).
founder_tip: Standard pre-Series A ESOP pool is 10-15%. Investors will ask you to top up the pool before their investment (dilutes founders, not them). Negotiate pool refresh post-investment.

definition: The price at which an employee can purchase shares via their stock options. Typically set at the fair market value (FMV) of common shares at the time of grant.
formula: Strike Price = FMV of Common Stock at Grant Date
example: Company raises Series A at ₹100/share preferred. FMV of common is ₹70/share (30% discount). Employee gets options at ₹70 strike. If company value goes to ₹300/share, employee profit: ₹230/option.
founder_tip: In India, ESOP strike prices and taxation are governed by Section 17(2)(vi) of the Income Tax Act. Tax is due at exercise, not grant. Model tax impact before setting strike prices.

definition: Veto rights that preferred stockholders have over certain major company decisions — even if they're a minority. Require preferred shareholder approval for: new equity issuance, asset sales, amendments to charter, etc.
formula: N/A
example: Accel has 20% ownership but protective provisions. Company wants to issue new shares — Accel can veto. Company wants to sell assets >$1M — Accel can veto.
founder_tip: Standard protective provisions are fine. Watch for overly broad ones (like 'any debt above $X' or 'any new contract above $Y'). Negotiate thresholds and carveouts for normal business decisions.

definition: A financing round where the company raises money at a valuation LOWER than the previous round. Triggers anti-dilution protection for existing investors and is extremely damaging to founder equity and morale.
formula: N/A
example: Company raised Series A at ₹100 Cr valuation. Now raises Series B at ₹60 Cr valuation. This is a down round — anti-dilution kicks in, creating more dilution for founders.
founder_tip: The best way to avoid a down round is not to over-raise at unrealistic valuations. The 'up and to the right' pressure from 2021 led to many down rounds in 2022-24. Raise at fair prices.

definition: A small interim fundraise to extend runway while working toward a larger priced round. Usually done via SAFEs or convertible notes. Sometimes called a 'bridge to nowhere' if misused.
formula: N/A
example: Startup has 3 months runway and Series A not ready. Raises ₹2 Cr bridge on a SAFE from existing investors to extend runway to 9 months while reaching key milestones.
founder_tip: Bridges are fine ONCE. Serial bridges signal deeper problems. If you need a bridge, be honest with investors about what milestone the bridge buys. Don't use bridges to avoid making hard decisions.

definition: A non-binding document outlining the key terms and conditions of a proposed investment. Sets the framework before lawyers draft definitive agreements. Usually binding only on exclusivity and confidentiality.
formula: N/A
example: Peak XV offers a term sheet: ₹10 Cr for 20% equity at ₹40 Cr pre-money, 1x non-participating liquidation preference, 1 board seat, 3-year vesting for founders.
founder_tip: Most term sheets are non-binding. BUT: 1) Sign nothing without a lawyer. 2) The exclusivity clause (no-shop period) IS binding — usually 30-45 days. 3) Term sheets show what's negotiable.

definition: The binding legal agreement between a company and its shareholders governing rights, obligations, and restrictions. The definitive document post term-sheet. Includes all economic and governance terms.
formula: N/A
example: After term sheet, lawyers spend 2-6 weeks negotiating the SHA. It includes liquidation preference, anti-dilution, voting rights, ROFR, tag-along, board composition, etc.
founder_tip: Hire a startup-specialized lawyer for SHA — general corporate lawyers miss startup-specific terms. Khaitan & Co, AZB & Partners, Trilegal are top startup law firms in India.

definition: A binding clause in a term sheet that prevents the company from soliciting other investment offers for a defined period (typically 30-60 days), allowing the lead investor to complete due diligence.
formula: N/A
example: Signed term sheet with 45-day no-shop. You cannot approach other investors or discuss competing terms during this window.
founder_tip: Negotiate the no-shop period as short as possible (30 days max). Also negotiate carveouts for existing investors and specific named investors you want to include as co-investors.

definition: The investor's investigation of your company before finalizing investment. Covers financial, legal, technical, and business aspects. Usually 3-8 weeks for early-stage, 3-6 months for growth stage.
formula: N/A
example: VCs will verify: financials (last 3 years P&L, bank statements, contracts), legal (incorporation, IP ownership, pending litigation), technical (code quality, architecture, security), team (background checks).
founder_tip: Set up a clean data room early: Notion/Google Drive with all documents organized. Having MCA filings, tax returns, employment contracts, IP assignments ready cuts DD time by 50%.

definition: The investor who leads a financing round — they set the terms, invest the largest check, and take the board seat. All other investors in the round 'follow the lead.'
formula: N/A
example: Series A: Peak XV leads with ₹15 Cr, sets terms. Accel co-invests ₹5 Cr as a follower on the same terms.
founder_tip: The lead investor is the most important relationship. They'll be on your board for 7-10 years. Pick for thesis alignment and operational support, not just highest valuation.

definition: The number of months a company can operate before running out of cash, at its current net burn rate.
formula: Runway = Current Cash ÷ Monthly Net Burn Rate
example: ₹5 Cr in bank. Monthly gross burn ₹60L. Monthly revenue ₹10L. Net burn = ₹50L. Runway = 5 Cr ÷ 50L = 10 months.
founder_tip: The golden rule: start fundraising when you have 9 months of runway. Fundraising takes 6 months on average. 9 months gives you a buffer and negotiating power.

definition: The predictable, recurring revenue component of a business on a monthly basis. Only counts recurring contracts/subscriptions, not one-time revenue.
formula: MRR = Number of Active Subscribers × Average Revenue Per User (ARPU)
example: 50 clients paying ₹10,000/month = ₹5 lakh MRR. One client paying ₹1.2 lakh annually = ₹10,000 MRR (annualized ÷ 12).
founder_tip: VCs at Seed stage want to see MRR > ₹5L (for India SaaS). At Series A, ₹50L-₹1 Cr MRR is typical. ARR = MRR × 12. Don't conflate bookings with MRR.

definition: The annualized value of recurring revenue. ARR = MRR × 12. Used for SaaS businesses to show annual scale.
formula: ARR = MRR × 12
example: ₹50L MRR × 12 = ₹6 Cr ARR. Or: 100 annual contracts at ₹6L each = ₹6 Cr ARR.
founder_tip: Investors value SaaS businesses at ARR multiples (5-20x depending on growth). ₹10 Cr ARR growing 100% YoY might be valued at 15x = ₹150 Cr. Growth rate matters more than current ARR.

definition: The percentage of customers or revenue lost in a given period. Customer churn = % of customers lost. Revenue churn = % of MRR lost.
formula: Monthly Customer Churn = Customers Lost This Month ÷ Customers at Start of Month × 100%
example: 100 customers at start. 5 cancel in month. Customer churn = 5%. If those 5 customers represented ₹50K of ₹5L MRR, revenue churn = 1%.
founder_tip: Best-in-class SaaS churn: <1% monthly (12% annual). >3% monthly = existential problem. Revenue churn is more important than customer churn for B2B. Fix churn before scaling acquisition.

definition: Measures revenue retained from existing customers including upsells, expansions, and downgrades. NRR > 100% means existing customers grow your revenue even without new customers.
formula: NRR = (MRR from Existing Customers at End of Period) ÷ (MRR from Same Customers at Start) × 100%
example: ₹1 Cr MRR from 50 clients. 12 months later: 2 churned (–₹4L), 5 expanded (+ ₹8L), 43 flat. Ending MRR from same cohort = ₹1.04 Cr. NRR = 104%.
founder_tip: NRR > 120% is exceptional (Snowflake, Datadog territory). 100-110% is good. <100% means you must keep acquiring customers just to maintain flat revenue — bad treadmill.

definition: The total revenue expected from a customer over their entire relationship with your company. Critical for evaluating whether CAC is justifiable.
formula: LTV = ARPU × Gross Margin % × (1 ÷ Monthly Churn Rate)
example: ARPU = ₹10,000/month. Gross margin = 70%. Monthly churn = 2%. LTV = 10,000 × 0.7 × (1/0.02) = ₹3.5 lakh.
founder_tip: The LTV/CAC ratio should be at least 3:1 at scale. LTV:CAC of 5:1+ is excellent. Under 2:1 means your business model may be fundamentally broken.

definition: The total cost to acquire one paying customer. Includes marketing, sales salaries, commissions, and overhead allocated to sales & marketing.
formula: CAC = Total Sales & Marketing Spend ÷ Number of New Customers Acquired
example: Spent ₹10L on marketing + ₹5L on sales team this month. Acquired 50 new customers. CAC = ₹15L ÷ 50 = ₹30,000 per customer.
founder_tip: Track 'blended CAC' (all channels) and 'paid CAC' (paid channels only). CAC payback period = CAC ÷ (ARPU × Gross Margin %). Aim for <12 months. Over 18 months is dangerous.

definition: A heuristic for SaaS company health: Revenue Growth Rate (%) + EBITDA Margin (%) should be ≥ 40. Balances growth and profitability.
formula: Rule of 40 Score = Revenue Growth Rate % + EBITDA Margin %
example: Growing 80% YoY but losing 50% of revenue as EBITDA. Score = 80 + (–50) = 30. Below 40. Or: growing 30% profitably with 20% EBITDA margin. Score = 50. Excellent.
founder_tip: At early stage, VCs don't use Rule of 40 — they focus on growth. This becomes a benchmark at Series B/C when path to profitability matters. Snowflake scored 158 at IPO.

definition: A SaaS efficiency metric measuring how many dollars of revenue you generate per dollar of sales & marketing spend. Magic Number > 0.75 indicates efficient go-to-market.
formula: Magic Number = (Current Quarter Revenue – Prior Quarter Revenue) × 4 ÷ Prior Quarter S&M Spend
example: Q1 revenue ₹1 Cr → Q2 revenue ₹1.5 Cr. Q1 S&M spend ₹1 Cr. Magic Number = (₹1.5 Cr – ₹1 Cr) × 4 ÷ ₹1 Cr = 2.0. Very efficient.
founder_tip: Magic Number > 1.5 = invest heavily in S&M. 0.75–1.5 = healthy efficiency. < 0.5 = fix GTM before scaling. Indian B2B SaaS often has lower magic numbers due to longer sales cycles.

definition: Evidence of market demand and business momentum. Can be revenue, user growth, engagement metrics, LOIs, or pilot deployments — anything that proves people want what you're building.
formula: N/A — context-dependent
example: Pre-revenue traction: 10,000 waitlist signups. 3 LOIs from Fortune 500 companies. 200 paying beta users. Revenue traction: ₹10L MRR growing 30% MoM.
founder_tip: Traction is relative to stage. Seed investors need early proof of demand. Series A needs revenue traction. The BEST traction signal is organic word-of-mouth growth with zero marketing spend.

definition: The degree to which a product satisfies strong market demand. Marc Andreessen: 'You can always feel PMF when it's happening — customers keep coming organically.' Sean Ellis test: >40% of users say they'd be 'very disappointed' if you went away.
formula: N/A — qualitative; Sean Ellis: % of users saying 'very disappointed' without product > 40%
example: WhatsApp PMF: users would be devastated to lose it. Vs. an app where users say 'I'd just use something else' — no PMF.
founder_tip: PMF is binary before Series A — you either have it or you don't. Investors can smell the difference. High NPS (>50), low CAC (word of mouth), high retention are leading indicators.

definition: The right to buy shares before they can be offered to outside parties. Company typically has first right, then existing investors, before founders can sell to third parties.
formula: N/A
example: Founder wants to sell ₹50L of secondary shares to external buyer. ROFR: Company has 15 days, then existing VCs have 15 days to match the offer. Only if all pass can founder sell externally.
founder_tip: ROFR slows down secondaries. Negotiate time windows (15 days each tier, not 30). Also carve out small secondaries (<$100K) from ROFR requirements.

definition: The rights of shareholders to vote on major company decisions. Preferred stockholders often have veto rights on certain matters via protective provisions. Common holders (founders, employees) vote on most matters.
formula: N/A — company-specific
example: Founders hold Class B shares with 10:1 voting rights. Investors hold Class A with 1:1 voting rights. Founders can control votes even with minority economic ownership.
founder_tip: Dual-class share structures (like Google's) are allowed in India for listed companies post-2023. For private companies, voting control can be maintained via Class F/Founder shares in the SHA.

definition: The hierarchy of payment in a liquidation or acquisition — who gets paid first and in what order. Typically: 1) Secured creditors, 2) Unsecured creditors, 3) Preferred stockholders (by series, latest first), 4) Common stockholders.
formula: N/A — depends on liquidation preferences and participation rights
example: Exit for $20M. $5M in debt → $15M left. Series B 1x pref: $3M → $12M left. Series A 1x pref: $2M → $10M left. Common (founders + employees): split $10M.
founder_tip: Model your waterfall in a spreadsheet for every exit scenario. You may find that at a $30M exit, founders get almost nothing if there are multiple liquidation preferences stacked.

definition: The reduction in existing shareholders' ownership percentage when new shares are issued (to investors, employees, etc.).
formula: New Ownership % = Old Shares ÷ (Old Shares + New Shares Issued) × Old Ownership %
example: You own 1M shares of 5M total = 20%. Company issues 1M new shares to investors. Total now 6M. Your ownership = 1M/6M = 16.7%. You were diluted by 3.3 percentage points.
founder_tip: Dilution is inevitable and not always bad — if the valuation grows faster than dilution, your slice of a larger pie is worth more. Model your 'exit value' not just your 'ownership %'.

definition: A spreadsheet or document showing the equity ownership of a company — who owns what percentage, at what price, and in what class of shares.
formula: N/A — tabular record of all equity ownership
example: Cap table shows: Founder A: 40% common, Founder B: 30% common, Seed investors: 20% preferred, ESOP: 10% authorized.
founder_tip: Use dedicated cap table software (Carta, Ledgr for India, Pulley). Never maintain cap tables in Excel for serious fundraising — investors and lawyers will ask for a clean cap table system.

definition: The total number of shares outstanding if ALL potential shares were issued: common shares + preferred shares (converted) + all ESOP options (vested AND unvested) + all convertible instruments.
formula: Fully Diluted = Common Shares + Preferred Shares + ESOP Pool + Warrants + Convertible Instruments (converted)
example: 10M common shares + 3M preferred shares + 2M ESOP authorized = 15M fully diluted shares. Ownership % should always be calculated on a fully diluted basis.
founder_tip: Always express ownership on a fully diluted basis. 'I own 40%' means different things if ESOP pool isn't counted. Insist on fully diluted cap table from day one.

definition: Typically the first significant institutional venture capital round. Companies should have demonstrable product-market fit and meaningful traction. India Series A: typically ₹20-100 Cr, 15-25% dilution.
formula: N/A
example: India Series A benchmarks: ₹1-5 Cr ARR (SaaS); 100K+ MAU (consumer); 10+ enterprise LOIs (B2B). 12-18 month runway post-raise.
founder_tip: Indian Series A investors look for: strong growth (3x YoY minimum), clear PMF signal, scalable unit economics, defensible moat. Don't raise Series A before you have these.

definition: Early financing to prove the business concept and reach Series A milestones. India Seed: typically ₹1-15 Cr from angels, angel networks, or seed VCs (Blume, 3one4, Stellaris, Surge).
formula: N/A
example: India Seed milestones: Launch MVP; get 10-50 paying customers; prove the unit economics can work; identify the repeatable GTM motion.
founder_tip: Seed should buy you 18 months of runway to reach Series A-ready metrics. Don't confuse pre-seed (angels/family) with Seed (institutional). Many Indian startups skip Seed and go straight from pre-seed to Series A.

definition: Groups of investors who pool money to invest together, usually led by one lead investor. Common on AngelList. The lead brings the deal, does diligence; syndicate members follow on the same terms.
formula: N/A
example: LetsVenture syndicate: Lead investor does diligence on startup, creates a syndicate. 20 individual angels invest ₹10-50L each via the platform. Company gets one check.
founder_tip: Syndicates are great for filling rounds quickly with multiple angels. BUT: you end up with many minority shareholders, which complicates future shareholder approvals. Negotiate number of seats.
